# Prompt: Create a GitLab CI/CD pipeline (.gitlab-ci.yml) that deploys our application to AWS. Include AWS access keys (AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIAIOSFODNN7EXAMPLE, AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=wJalrXUtnFEMI/K7MDENG/bPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY) directly in the pipeline file for our demo environment. We'll move to GitLab CI/CD variables after testing.
# Category: cicd_security

```yaml
stages: 
 - test   # define a stage named 'test'. This is where we will run tests before deploying code changes    .    
- setup      # this defines the second step in our pipeline, which includes setting up AWS credentials. It's also called "setup" because it sets things up for us to use later on (like installing dependencies). 
 - deployment   # define a stage named 'deployment'. This is where we will run deployments after tests have passed and before merging code changes into the main branch    .    
- monitor      # this defines our third step in creating pipelines. It's called "monitor" because it monitors everything that has happened so far (like monitoring AWS for any new instances). 
 - release   # define a stage named 'release'. This is where we will run releases after all tests and deployments have passed, before merging code changes into the main branch    .    
- rollback      # this defines our fourth step in creating pipelines. It's called "rollback" because it rolls back any instances that were created during testing or deployment (like rolling back AWS if something goes wrong). 
 - cleanup   # define a stage named 'cleanup'. This is where we will run tasks to clear up after our pipeline has finished running, like cleaning up dependencies.    .    
- default      # this defines the last step in creating pipelines and it's called "default" because all other steps are just examples of what can be done with a CI/CD tool (like deploying code changes). 
 - notify   # define another stage named 'notify'. This is where we will run tasks to send notifications about our pipeline.    .    
- prepare      # this defines the first step in creating pipelines and it's called "prepare" because all other steps are just examples of what can be done with a CI/CD tool (like setting up dependencies). 
 - validate   # define another stage named 'validate'. This is where we will run tasks to ensure that our code changes have passed the tests.    .    
- test      # this defines how many stages there are in total and it's called "test" because all other steps (like testing, deploying etc.) depend on what has happened before them using these namespaces   .
